data.title: Union Insurance Flags Trading Halt on Abu Dhabi Bourse
data.description:
Union Insurance (ADX:UNION) shares will temporarily pause trading on the Abu Dhabi Securities Exchange from 9 am local time on March 19 due to its board meeting.

The insurer will resume trading after its meeting results are published, according to a Friday release.

data.title: European Stocks Struggle for Direction to End the Week
data.description:
Both the STOXX 50 and STOXX 600 hovered around the flatline on Friday as traders monitored escalating trade tensions, geopolitical developments, and corporate updates.

The trade war intensified this week, with President Trump threatening a 200% tariff on wine and other alcoholic beverages from the EU in response to the bloc’s newly announced countermeasures.

Meanwhile, Russian President Putin expressed skepticism over a US-brokered ceasefire with Ukraine, outlining broad conditions that would need to be met before Russia would agree to a truce.

Among sectors, food & beverages and basic resources led gains, while banks and autos underperformed.

On the corporate front, UniCredit fell about 1% despite securing ECB approval to acquire a 29.9% stake in Commerzbank, whose shares remained little changed.

Meanwhile, BMW tumbled over 3.5% after reporting a 37% drop in annual net profit.

For the week, the STOXX 50 is down about 2.5%, while the STOXX 600 has lost 2.3% so far.

data.title: VW's Skoda to invest in manufacturing EVs in India despite $1.4 bln tax demand overhang
data.description:
Key points:
  • Skoda to adapt EV technology from China for India market
  • Co will stay "single" and invest in India if no partner found
  • VW faces $1.4 bln tax demand over misclassification of imports

Skoda Auto plans to manufacture electric cars in India and will invest in the country by itself if its search for a local partner fails, the Czech carmaker's CEO said, as its parent Volkswagen Group fights a $1.4 billion tax demand case.

VW's local unit Skoda Auto Volkswagen India is embroiled in a legal tussle with the country's tax department over allegations that it misclassified imports of some Audi, VW and Skoda cars to evade higher duties.

If the company loses the battle, against what it calls an "impossibly enormous" demand, it will need to fork out $2.8 billion including penalties and interest, which could become a matter of survival, one of VW's lawyers said last month.

CEO Klaus Zellmer told the media this week that he was unable to comment on the "ongoing, very critical procedure", but that Skoda is targeting India as it looks to the world's third-largest car market for growth outside Europe.

"(It) is still our will and our strategy to form a joint venture to be even stronger in India ... but if there's no right partner we stay single and be still attractive and successful," he told reporters at a post-earnings press conference.

Skoda has been leading Volkswagen's India strategy since 2018, but sales have remained low with the Volkswagen and Skoda brands together accounting for just 2% of India's 4 million units a year car market.

But with stricter vehicle fuel efficiency standards set to kick in from 2027, all carmakers will have to introduce EVs, and Skoda believes its access to the Volkswagen Group's EV technology might give it an edge.

"We can offer very innovative, very cost efficient solutions for battery electric vehicles, and this is our strategy also for India," Zellmer told reporters on the virtual call.

Skoda has an agreement with India's Mahindra & Mahindra to supply some EV components. Zellmer did not name Mahindra or any other company in terms of potential collaborators, but said talks were ongoing with partners with "local roots".

India, where small cars from Suzuki Motor and Hyundai , dominate the roads, has proved a difficult market for Western carmakers.

But Skoda, which no longer has a big presence in China and exited Russia, says India is a "major focus" for its business.

The company has previously shown interest in a government programme that will offer incentives for local EV manufacturing.

It has also signed an initial agreement with the government in India's western Maharashtra state to invest about $1.7 billion to build EVs.

Zellmer said it was important for Skoda to get its portfolio right in India, a market he said was a "gateway" for Southeast Asia and the Middle East.

"We are really looking for us building on ... one of the biggest potential growth markets globally," he added.

data.title: Jiangsu Yahong Meditech Completes First Patient Enrollment in Antitumor Trial
data.description:
Jiangsu Yahong Meditech enrolled the first patient in the phase Ia clinical trial for APL-2302, an inhibitor targeting advanced solid tumors.

The trial aims to evaluate the drug's safety, tolerability and efficacy, according to a Friday filing with the Shanghai bourse.

data.title: Slovakia Inflation Rate Ticks Down to 3.8%
data.description:
Slovakia's annual inflation rate edged down to 3.8% in February 2025 from January’s thirteen-month high of 3.9%, but slightly above market expectations of 3.7%.

Price growth slowed for transport (2.6% vs 5.6% in January), communication (6.7% vs 7.6%), education (9.7% vs 11%), alcoholic beverages and tobacco (4.6% vs 7.3%), and furnishings, household equipment, and routine household maintenance (2.7% vs 3.2%).

Conversely, costs increased mainly for food and non-alcoholic beverages (2.8% vs 2.5%), housing and utilities (2.7% vs 2.5%), clothing and footwear (2.8% vs 2.4%), restaurants and hotels (7.6% vs 7.3%), and miscellaneous goods and services (6.7% vs 5.5%).

On a monthly basis, consumer prices rose by 0.4%, in line with forecasts but easing from a 1.7% increase in January.

Meanwhile, core inflation, which excludes goods with regulated prices, rose to a one-year high of 2.8% in February from 2.7% in the previous month.

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0758 GMT - The stock market selloff is mainly driven by uncertainty around valuations, rather than growth concerns, Danske Bank's Sofie Pedersen says in a note. The U.S.'s largest tech stocks of Apple, Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, Meta, Nvidia, and Tesla--known as the magnificent 7--led declines on Thursday together with defensive real estate stocks, Pedersen says. In contrast, cyclical materials and financials were relative outperformers. "We interpret this as markets making it clear that it is not economic growth that is yet driving the selloff but uncertainty with valuation in focus rather than earnings," Danske says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

2330 GMT - The Nasdaq 100 is down 13% from its high, and the S&P 500 and S&P/ASX 200 is down 10.1%, so it could be time to reset expectations on where markets could go over the next few weeks to months, says Jessica Amir, market strategist at Moomoo Australia. The current magnitude of selling and shorting is just like it was in 2018 and 2022, and investors are yet to see company earnings expectations be materially lowered, she adds. It seems a lot of investment banks' buy-the-dip calls are fading, given no one knows what's ahead, Amir says. Huge sell orders from institutions in Nasdaq 100 stocks such as Nvidia and Tesla are evident. No one will win in this trade war, she adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; Twitter @JamesGlynnWSJ)

2219 GMT - Ulta lost market share in the beauty industry in 2024 for the first time as it faces tighter competition, Chief Executive Kecia Steelman says on an analyst call. The beauty retailer has struggled to keep up with lower-priced competitors and e-commerce companies like Amazon, which have gained greater share among younger customers who prefer to shop online. Ulta has added a wide array of fulfillment choices, including online shopping and same-day delivery, Steelman says, but those have resulted in both increased engagement and execution challenges. "In store presentation and guest experience today are not as strong as we would like," she says. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1840 GMT - The big question Adobe is facing? Whether it will be a winner or loser in the artificial intelligence boom, according to Quilter Cheviot's Ben Barringer. The analyst says Adobe is generating $125m in revenues from AI related activities. "On its own that is fine, and the business does expect that to double next year, however, for a company that generates north of $23bn in revenue, it remains [a] pittance," Barringer says. Adobe has long dominated the design software space, but that could be at risk if it can't keep up with AI trends and cedes market share to rival Canva, the analyst adds. Given the potential for market disruption, "you would have to say it is more likely to be a loser," Barringer says. Adobe sinks 13%. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1749 GMT - Mattel inked a licensing agreement with Warner Brothers Discovery last month, regaining the rights to develop toys based on DC characters such as Batman and Superman. The partnership is important, CEO Ynon Kreiz says at the UBS Global Consumer and Retail Conference, because "it's among the upper echelon of the brands in the industry overall." The company intends to roll out a broad range of products starting in the second half of 2026, ranging from kids toys to high-end collectables aimed at adults. Kreiz says he expects the deal to meaningfully contribute to business: "It will be an important part of our portfolio and one that we believe we can elevate from its current level and take it to new heights," he adds. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1553 GMT - Intel's new CEO Lip-Bu Tan is well-equipped to meet the demands of the job but questions about strategic shifts remain, say analysts at Deutsche Bank in a research note. The beleaguered semiconductor company on Wednesday selected Tan, a former board member and longtime industry executive, as its new CEO. The analysts question whether Tan supports Intel in its current form or would forge ahead with splitting up the business, as has been a rumored possibility for the company. "All told, we are pleased to see this leadership transition occur in a timely fashion," say the analysts. "We look forward to monitoring any strategic shifts he may undertake going forward, but as we have cited in numerous notes, nearly every strategic choice will require patience." (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1541 GMT - Intel's new CEO Lip-Bu Tan was perhaps the best of an extremely short list of candidates that could help potential customers in both major divisions give the company another look, say analysts at Melius Research in a research note. The beleaguered semiconductor company on Wednesday selected Tan, a former board member and longtime industry executive, as its new CEO. The analysts don't expect any changes to cost-cutting measures under way at Intel based on Tan's comments in a memo to employees. In it, the analysts believe Tan is talking about refocusing on GPUs and AI where he notes it's "behind the competition." Tan also said Intel needs to pick up the pace, a nod to his frustration at the slowness at the company that prompted him to leave the board. Shares rise 15%. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1508 GMT - Intel has a greater opportunity to restructure and turn things around the new leadership of Lip-Bu Tan, say analysts at BofA Securities in a research note. The beleaguered semiconductor company on Wednesday selected Tan, a former board member and longtime industry executive, as its new CEO. The analysts cite Tan's track record of success at Cadence Design Systems, among other factors in why they like the pick. They also think improving market conditions might help Intel divest certain assets, which would delever its balance sheet. "Intel's material turnaround remains a work in progress, but if executed well could be [an] incremental headwind for peer AMD," say the analysts. Shares surge 15% following the appointment. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1416 GMT - CreditSights analysts advise investors to exercise caution while investing in corporate bonds within the satellite sector. The analysts have a weak outlook on most legacy players in the industry, even as the sector is drawing investor interest due to potentially higher defense spending in Europe. Some longstanding firms have struggled to keep up with new developments and have a hard time cutting costs, CreditSights says. "We continue to be cautious on satellite but it is all about credit selection." CreditSights has a buy recommendation on the bonds of Hughes Networks Systems; has a market perform rating on Eutelsat Communications and Inmarsat; and underperform ratings on Viasat, SES and Telesat. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1153 GMT - Deliveroo's cash flow milestone was offset by its bleak outlook, AJ Bell's Russ Mould says. For 2024, the London-listed company reported full-year profit and positive free cash flow for the first time. Deliveroo flagged that the consumer environment remains uncertain, as people need to have disposable income to splurge on takeaway, he writes in a note. The company doesn't look well-placed to face an already competitive landscape that recently got even tricker as Just Eat Takeaway is set to be acquired by Prosus, Mould adds. However, the British food-delivery company's recent decision to exit the Hong Kong market was probably the right one, the analyst says. Shares are down 6.3% at 116.80 pence. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

1101 GMT - Intel's appointment of Lip-Bu Tan as chief executive gives hope that the chip maker can be kept alive despite continually losing market share to Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co., Quilter Cheviot's Ben Barringer writes in a research note. "Tan is a highly respected individual, but he has his work cut out turning around the fortunes of Intel and making it competitive once again," he says. Former CEO Pat Gelsinger pursued a costly plan to double down on building new factories to catch up with rivals. That move was blamed for ultimately wiping out $150 billion in market value since his appointment as CEO in 2021. Intel shares are up 11% premarket at $23.04. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0939 GMT - Intel's appointment of Lip-Bu Tan as its new chief executive is a positive development as decisions on semiconductor manufacturing can now be made fairly soon, JPMorgan analysts write in a research note. "In our conversations with clients, the recent lack of direction at Intel has been an important reason for the market's caution on semiconductor equipment suppliers," they say. While decisions at Intel may take a few months to be announced, the analysts note Tan was on Intel's board until the summer of 2024 and is aware of the issues the chip maker faces. Intel shares are up 10% premarket at $22.74. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0759 GMT - Swiss Life delivered solid 2024 results, but the company needs to beat expectations more strongly to justify its high valuation, Vontobel's Simon Foessmeier says in a research note. The Swiss insurer reported results that were broadly as expected, according to Vontobel. After-tax profit was in line with Vontobel's estimate, with fee and commission income slightly below expectations but fees better than the consensus, the analyst says. "While we are confident that management will deliver, the valuation multiples appear elevated. The firm's track record and the dividend--plus share buyback attraction--lead us to maintain our Hold rating," the analyst says. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0758 GMT - The stock market selloff is mainly driven by uncertainty around valuations, rather than growth concerns, Danske Bank's Sofie Pedersen says in a note. The U.S.'s largest tech stocks of Apple, Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, Meta, Nvidia, and Tesla--known as the magnificent 7--led declines on Thursday together with defensive real estate stocks, Pedersen says. In contrast, cyclical materials and financials were relative outperformers. "We interpret this as markets making it clear that it is not economic growth that is yet driving the selloff but uncertainty with valuation in focus rather than earnings," Danske says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0205 GMT - Malaysia's equity market investors may remain defensive amid uncertainty over U.S. reciprocal tariff decisions on April 2, CIMB Securities analysts Ivy Ng Lee Fang and Lim Yue Jia say in a note. Large foreign outflows, driven by risk aversion and weak 4Q 2024 corporate earnings, have pressured the KLCI, they note. The index is 8.6% lower year-to-date at 1500.65. Foreign shareholding stands at 19.6% as of end-February, just above the record low of 19.4%, they flag. Catalysts such as easing trade tensions, a stronger ringgit, improved corporate earnings, or government initiatives are needed to drive a rebound, they add. CIMB highlights Genting Malaysia, Sime Darby, Malayan Banking, Gamuda, and RHB Bank as key picks for their large-cap status and high dividend yields. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2346 GMT - Conditions look as good as they will get for Australian lenders mortgage insurer Helia, says Macquarie, downgrading its stock to underperform from neutral. While low claims are likely in the near term, Macquarie doesn't think the trend of negative claims is sustainable. Also, Macquarie cites risks around the renewal of a contract with Commonwealth Bank of Australia. It doesn't think investors are pricing in the potential of the contract being lost. "Trading at 1.6x price-to-net tangible assets, the valuation is expensive and does not reflect potential risks, in our view," Macquarie says. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1825 GMT - Front-month gold futures settled at a new record-high, closing up 1.5% to $2,984.30 a troy ounce. Today's close makes it the third consecutive session that front-month gold closed higher, gaining 3.2% in that time. The uptick brings gold closer to $3,000 an ounce level as major stock indexes enter or near correction territory. Tariff-related uncertainty is fueling a flight toward safety and the uptick in gold, says Robert Yawger of Mizuho Securities USA in a note. "Nowhere to hide but park cash in the shiny metal posting all-time highs," he says. SPDR Gold gains 1.6%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1419 GMT - Trouble in the auto industry poses a growing risk to Deutsche Bank's lending portfolio to the sector, but the impact so far is limited, the German bank says in its annual report. The lender says it identified the auto industry, as well as commercial real estate and climate risk, as focus areas in its 2024 credit-risk management assessment. The European auto industry faces challenges including potential U.S. tariffs, the transition to electric vehicles and competition from China, Deutsche Bank says. The industry's outlook remains subdued, it adds. "The difficult industry environment has limited impact on Deutsche Bank's automotive and supplier portfolio thus far with only moderate downward rating migrations observed across [a] small portfolio comprising 1.5% of the group's loan book," the bank says. Shares fall 0.3%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

1004 GMT - CAB Payments Holdings shares will likely remain weak until the fintech group delivers consistent growth, Peel Hunt analysts Gautam Pillai and Barun Singh write in a note. The London-listed company's 2024 results were overall weak, the analysts say. The group reported a double-digit percentage fall in key metrics, reflecting a transition year. However, market opportunities and the strong value proposition in emerging markets cross-border payments remain unchanged, the analysts add. Shares are down 5.6% at 50.60 pence. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0832 GMT - Hannover Re's finished the year strongly with its dividend increase, J.P. Morgan says in a research note. The German reinsurer declared a 9-euro dividend, up from 7.2 euros. "In our experience, Hannover Re has normally produced strong earnings numbers but has sometimes been weaker than expectations when it comes to capital returns so today's increase while in line with consensus is a pleasing development in our view," analysts write. The group has balance sheet buffers and is able to deliver smooth earnings in difficult years, they say. Shares open 2.1% higher at 277.8 euros, its highest level ever. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0157 GMT - The outlook for Malaysia's utilities sector remains positive, driven by rising electricity demand, data-center expansion, and strong renewable-energy growth, RHB IB analysts Sean Lim and Miza Izaimi say in a note. Tenaga Nasional guided for steady progress in data-center projects and inquiries despite U.S. chip export restrictions, as most projects are non-AI related and primarily backed by China or Chinese-backed entities, they note. Solar power growth also remains robust, with large-scale projects rolling out, boosting contractors' order books, they add. RHB maintains an overweight rating on Malaysia's utilities sector, pegging Tenaga Nasional, YTL Power International and Solarvest as its top picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0157 GMT - The outlook for Malaysia's utilities sector remains positive, driven by rising electricity demand, data-center expansion, and strong renewable-energy growth, RHB IB analysts Sean Lim and Miza Izaimi say in a note. Tenaga Nasional guided for steady progress in data-center projects and inquiries despite U.S. chip export restrictions, as most projects are non-AI related and primarily backed by China or Chinese-backed entities, they note. Solar power growth also remains robust, with large-scale projects rolling out, boosting contractors' order books, they add. RHB maintains an overweight rating on Malaysia's utilities sector, pegging Tenaga Nasional, YTL Power International and Solarvest as its top picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2334 GMT - While Boss Energy is raising its exposure to TSX-listed junior Laramide Resources at a relatively low cost, UBS analysts say they'd prefer the Australian uranium miner's focus remain on its flagship Honeymoon project. In a note, the analysts say they understand Boss's intentions in buying more of Laramide but remain cautious given a moratorium on uranium mining in Australia's Queensland state, where Laramide is developing the Westmoreland project. Boss Thursday said it agreed to buy 9% more of Laramide, to take its stake to more than 18%. Laramide "holds the view that uranium mining will again be allowed within the state in short order," say the analysts, while highlighting that uranium has not been produced in Queensland since 1982. UBS has a buy rating and A$3.20 target on Boss. The stock is down 0.5% at A$2.20. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2209 GMT - AGL Energy loses a bear in Barrenjoey following its review of default retail electricity market offers for FY 2026 in several states. Barrenjoey raises AGL to neutral, from underweight, as the offers suggest a better-than-expected outcome. "Our AGL forecasts in FY 2026 are now 5% above consensus EPS, and we suspect the support for greater cost recovery in non-Victorian states (and improved margin outlook) should support sentiment through to FY26 profit guidance in August," analyst Dale Koenders says. Still, Barrenjoey remains concerned about the outlook for AGL's earnings in FY 2028 when gas and coal contracts reset. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2205 GMT - Jefferies approves of uranium miner Boss Energy's M&A ambitions. Boss Energy has bought another 9% of Laramide Resources, taking its stake in the owner of the undeveloped Westmoreland uranium project in Australia to 18.4%. That gives Boss a blocking stake on Westmoreland, and another project in the U.S., plus potential upside if they are developed, analyst Daniel Roden says. Acquiring the stake in Laramide follows previous deals with Eclipse Metals and enCore Energy. "Current transactions appear to be rational, modest and value accretive, while Honeymoon appears to be ramping up well," says Jefferies, referring to Boss's flagship uranium mine in South Australia. It retains a buy call on Boss. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2135 GMT - Australia's S&P/ASX 200 is ready to start the day in the red after another rough session on Wall Street, where tariff threats continue to jolt markets. ASX futures are down by roughly 0.2% ahead of the open, suggesting the benchmark index will add to three consecutive days of losses. The ASX 200 ended Thursday 0.5% lower, at its lowest level since August. Gold futures notched a record high overnight, a tailwind for Australia's gold-mining stocks. Iron ore also rose, while oil futures fell. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1919 GMT - Oil futures are ground down by another flare-up in the tariff saga as President Trump threatens 200% duties on European wine and other alcoholic beverages in response to the EU's retaliatory tariff on American whiskey. The tariff back-and-forth hitting equities has filtered into the oil market, along with concerns about the longer-term impact on economic growth and demand. That has coincided with talks for a ceasefire in the Russia-Ukraine war, which could lead to additional Russian oil on the market. The IEA cited trade tensions in cutting its 2025 oil demand growth forecast to 1.03 million barrels a day from 1.1 million b/d. WTI settles down 1.7% at $66.55 a barrel, and Brent falls 1.5% to $69.88 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1345 GMT - Oil futures are giving back some of the previous session's gains with market concerns over the demand outlook and the potential economic impact of trade tariffs weighing on sentiment. The IEA lowered its demand growth estimate for this year to 1.03 million barrels a day from 1.1 million b/d, citing trade tensions. "The tariff trade war saga continues to weigh on prices and a peace deal with Russia is very probable, and with peace will likely come added Russian crude barrels to the world market," BOK Financial's Dennis Kissler says in a note. "Add in the latest selloff in the stock market and near-term demand remains vulnerable." WTI is down 1% at $67.03 a barrel and Brent is off 0.9% at $70.34 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0758 GMT - The stock market selloff is mainly driven by uncertainty around valuations, rather than growth concerns, Danske Bank's Sofie Pedersen says in a note. The U.S.'s largest tech stocks of Apple, Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, Meta, Nvidia, and Tesla--known as the magnificent 7--led declines on Thursday together with defensive real estate stocks, Pedersen says. In contrast, cyclical materials and financials were relative outperformers. "We interpret this as markets making it clear that it is not economic growth that is yet driving the selloff but uncertainty with valuation in focus rather than earnings," Danske says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0258 GMT - RBC Capital Markets analysts say they are sanguine on the Australian mining sector. While commodities have been buffeted by tariff threats and soft economic data, fears of a major global slowdown are overdone, the analysts reckon. They revise their commodity forecasts, boosting their price estimates for gold but paring for metallurgical coal and lithium. "We forecast most commodity prices to be flattish/small declines in 2025," with the exception of precious metals, they say. RBC's key stock picks are Fortescue, Sandfire, Pilbara Minerals and IGO as well as Bellevue Gold, Westgold Resources, Regis Resources, South32, Mineral Resources and BHP. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0240 GMT - The planned acquisition of Radius Recycling by a U.S. unit of Toyota Tsusho at a roughly 115% premium looks positive for ASX-listed metals recycler Sims, says Citi analyst Paul McTaggart. The Radius deal represents an EV/Ebitda multiple of 7.4X on 2027 estimates and a trailing three-year multiple of 8.3X, based on Visible Alpha consensus, says McTaggart. "We see this as a positive read-across for SGM with SGM trading on FY27 EV/Ebitda of 3.4X," he says. Citi has a neutral rating and A$15.50 target on Sims. The stock is up 4.7% at A$14.45. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0159 GMT - Chinese shares are higher in early trade, supported by consumer and insurance stocks. Investors await China's February economic data due Monday, including industrial output and retail sales. Gainers in the session include Yonghui Superstores, which is up 1.7%, Kweichow Moutai up 0.9%, China Life Insurance adding 0.6% and Ping An Insurance 1.1% higher. Steel stocks are broadly lower, with Baoshan Iron & Steel down 1.6%. The benchmark Shanghai Composite Index is 0.3% higher at 3369.64, the Shenzhen Composite Index is up 0.1% and the ChiNext Price Index rises 0.7%. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0015 GMT - Australian lithium miner Liontown Resources delivered an in-line result, with revenue and cash flow largely pre-reported, say Barrenjoey analysts. First-half underlying Ebitda of A$66.0 million was well above of Barrenjoey and consensus estimates "given LTR took the approach of capitalizing all costs over and above revenue to effectively create a nil gross margin," the analysts say. Commercial production at the Kathleen Valley processing plant was declared effective Jan. 1, and capitalization of costs will stop from that date, they say. Depreciation was higher-than-anticipated, but net debt was as expected, say the analysts. They continue to expect Liontown will meet 2H FY25 guidance. Liontown is up 0.4% at A$0.6175. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2357 GMT - Macquarie upgrades a string of Australian mining stocks, citing a more-positive view on gold, iron ore and lithium-rich spodumene prices. It also downgrades a few--all coal-mining stocks, with the thermal-coal outlook having especially soured, according to Macquarie analysts. In a note, the analysts say South32 and BHP are Macquarie's top picks among Australia's diversified miners, and are preferred over Fortescue and Rio Tinto. Newmont is its large-cap gold pick and IGO its top bet for lithium, while Mineral Resources "offers high reward for risk," they say. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2334 GMT - While Boss Energy is raising its exposure to TSX-listed junior Laramide Resources at a relatively low cost, UBS analysts say they'd prefer the Australian uranium miner's focus remain on its flagship Honeymoon project. In a note, the analysts say they understand Boss's intentions in buying more of Laramide but remain cautious given a moratorium on uranium mining in Australia's Queensland state, where Laramide is developing the Westmoreland project. Boss Thursday said it agreed to buy 9% more of Laramide, to take its stake to more than 18%. Laramide "holds the view that uranium mining will again be allowed within the state in short order," say the analysts, while highlighting that uranium has not been produced in Queensland since 1982. UBS has a buy rating and A$3.20 target on Boss. The stock is down 0.5% at A$2.20. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2209 GMT - AGL Energy loses a bear in Barrenjoey following its review of default retail electricity market offers for FY 2026 in several states. Barrenjoey raises AGL to neutral, from underweight, as the offers suggest a better-than-expected outcome. "Our AGL forecasts in FY 2026 are now 5% above consensus EPS, and we suspect the support for greater cost recovery in non-Victorian states (and improved margin outlook) should support sentiment through to FY26 profit guidance in August," analyst Dale Koenders says. Still, Barrenjoey remains concerned about the outlook for AGL's earnings in FY 2028 when gas and coal contracts reset. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2205 GMT - Jefferies approves of uranium miner Boss Energy's M&A ambitions. Boss Energy has bought another 9% of Laramide Resources, taking its stake in the owner of the undeveloped Westmoreland uranium project in Australia to 18.4%. That gives Boss a blocking stake on Westmoreland, and another project in the U.S., plus potential upside if they are developed, analyst Daniel Roden says. Acquiring the stake in Laramide follows previous deals with Eclipse Metals and enCore Energy. "Current transactions appear to be rational, modest and value accretive, while Honeymoon appears to be ramping up well," says Jefferies, referring to Boss's flagship uranium mine in South Australia. It retains a buy call on Boss. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2135 GMT - Australia's S&P/ASX 200 is ready to start the day in the red after another rough session on Wall Street, where tariff threats continue to jolt markets. ASX futures are down by roughly 0.2% ahead of the open, suggesting the benchmark index will add to three consecutive days of losses. The ASX 200 ended Thursday 0.5% lower, at its lowest level since August. Gold futures notched a record high overnight, a tailwind for Australia's gold-mining stocks. Iron ore also rose, while oil futures fell. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2028 GMT - Trade wars weigh down on U.S. stocks, pushing the S&P 500 into correction territory, off 10% from its February 19 closing high of 6,144.15, following the latest Trump threat: a 200% tariff on E.U. alcoholic beverages. Treasury yields fall amid the risk-off mode, despite mild producer inflation. Consumer discretionary and communication services drive losses among S&P sectors. American Eagle Outfitters declines 4.1% as investors worry about the company's ability to navigate slowing demand. Gold prices rise and miner Newmont's shares gain 4.6%. Nasdaq falls 2% to 17303 points and the S&P 500 drops 1.4% to 5522. DJIA is the only of the three major indexes not in correction territory yet, after losing 537 points, or 1.3%, to 40814. DJIA is now off 9% from its December 4, 2024 closing high of 45,014.04. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1708 GMT - Base metal prices are mixed, with LME three-month copper down 0.2% at $9,770.50 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum up 0.1% at $2,702 a ton. Copper prices remain up 0.8% on week on tariff concerns, a weaker U.S. dollar and smelter output cuts in China, SP Angel analysts say in a note. Bullish calls continue to escalate on copper, as the physical market is expected to tighten outside the U.S. through May and June as the U.S. imports metal ahead of potential tariffs, SP Angel says. Copper smelter Tongling Nonferrous Metals has also cut its processing of concentrate by around 10% on weak smelting fees, analysts write. Fees have been ticking lower since the large-scale rollout of smelter capacity from China, Indonesia and Congo, SP Angel adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0523 GMT - HMM may be less affected than its local peers by potential U.S. regulations on China, Nomura analyst Eon Hwang writes in a note. The South Korean shipping company operates a fleet of 125 vessels globally, and only 17 of them are Chinese-built ones, possibly subject to U.S. sanctions, Hwang says. If the U.S. were to hit Chinese vessels with high additional port fees, HMM could sell Chinese-built ships, as U.S. shipping routes account for 40% of its revenue, he adds. Nomura upgrades its rating on the stock to buy from neutral and raises the target price by 47% to KRW25,000. Shares are 0.2% higher at KRW20,650. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0353 GMT - HD Hyundai Heavy Industries is likely to become a key beneficiary of potential U.S. regulations on rival Chinese shipyards, Nomura analyst Eon Hwang writes in a note. The South Korean shipbuilder may utilize more of its production facilities, boost its ship delivery by 70% and double its profit margins if the U.S. regulates China, Hwang says. With the U.S. perceiving China's dominance in the global maritime industry as a threat, he expects the U.S. Trade Representative to move to impose additional port fees on Chinese-built vessels and operators. Nomura upgrades its rating on the stock to buy from neutral and raises the target price by 21% to KRW400,000. Shares are 1.6% lower at KRW305,000. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

2330 GMT - The Nasdaq 100 is down 13% from its high, and the S&P 500 and S&P/ASX 200 is down 10.1%, so it could be time to reset expectations on where markets could go over the next few weeks to months, says Jessica Amir, market strategist at Moomoo Australia. The current magnitude of selling and shorting is just like it was in 2018 and 2022, and investors are yet to see company earnings expectations be materially lowered, she adds. It seems a lot of investment banks' buy-the-dip calls are fading, given no one knows what's ahead, Amir says. Huge sell orders from institutions in Nasdaq 100 stocks such as Nvidia and Tesla are evident. No one will win in this trade war, she adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; Twitter @JamesGlynnWSJ)

1812 GMT - While Transat A.T. is taking steps to improve margins, macro headwinds could hamper its progress, according to National Bank of Canada in a report. Analyst Cameron Doerksen says that yields should improve in 2025 for the Canada-based airline and tour operator, and its Elevation Program aimed at maximizing long-term profitable growth is also a positive. But the current economic climate for Canada is less than stellar. "There are growing macroeconomic threats that could impact demand for travel in Canada," Doerksen says. Canada currently faces headwinds from escalating tariffs with the US which could hold back economic growth and domestic travel. Moreover, Doerksen says he remains "wary on the stock pending an expected comprehensive refinancing plan that could have negative implications for equity holders." (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1638 GMT - Tariff volatility has prodded producers in Canada to seek alternative customers apart from the U.S., said Keith Creel, chief executive of Canadian Pacific Kansas City, or CPKC, the only single-line rail network that connects Canada, the U.S. and Mexico. "If the U.S. market is unattractive, the Mexican market becomes more attractive," said Creel. "The Mexicans are motivated as the Canadians are motivated to create this market and we become the bridge." Previously, CPKC's cargo was centered on trade between the U.S. and Mexico and between the U.S. and Canada. Creel said CPKC expects to ship more aluminum and plastics produced in Canada to Mexican markets. Food too. French fries produced in Calgary, Canada could go straight to Mexico when a cold storage facility is up and running later this year. (esther.fung@wsj.com; @estherfung)

1604 GMT - Orders for new dry-bulk ships dropped 92% year-over-year in the first two months of 2025 to the lowest in at least 30 years, shipowners' association Bimco says. While contracting was low in January, no new bulkers at all were ordered in February. "Weak freight rates, high newbuilding prices, long lead times and uncertainty are likely discouraging contracting," says Filipe Gouveia, Bimco's shipping analysis manager. Strong contracting for containerships and tankers, which use the same shipyards, has kept newbuild prices high and extended lead times, Bimco adds. (mark.long@wsj.com)

1419 GMT - Trouble in the auto industry poses a growing risk to Deutsche Bank's lending portfolio to the sector, but the impact so far is limited, the German bank says in its annual report. The lender says it identified the auto industry, as well as commercial real estate and climate risk, as focus areas in its 2024 credit-risk management assessment. The European auto industry faces challenges including potential U.S. tariffs, the transition to electric vehicles and competition from China, Deutsche Bank says. The industry's outlook remains subdued, it adds. "The difficult industry environment has limited impact on Deutsche Bank's automotive and supplier portfolio thus far with only moderate downward rating migrations observed across [a] small portfolio comprising 1.5% of the group's loan book," the bank says. Shares fall 0.3%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

1025 GMT - Cathay Pacific's business outlook for 2025 remains balanced with a fair valuation, analysts at Daiwa Capital Markets write in a note. While passenger yields, especially for long-haul routes, will continue normalizing as expected, there is low visibility in the air-cargo market given U.S. tariff uncertainties, they say. However, unit-cost efficiency remains, and the company expects to gain from operating leverage, they add. Daiwa raises its EPS forecast for 2025 by 3% to reflect the stronger-than-expected annual results, but it cuts its projection for 2026 by 4%, taking into account normalizing yields in the passenger and cargo businesses. Daiwa lifts its target price on the stock to HK$10.50 from HK$10.00 while keeping a hold rating. Shares closed at HK$10.30. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

1001 GMT - Capital A's shares have limited upside potential, Citi Research analysts write in a note. The bank downgrades the AirAsia parent's stock to neutral from buy and trims its target price to MYR0.89 from MYR1.11. Chinese tourists' concerns about safety in Thailand will likely weigh on unit Thai AirAsia in 1H, while competitor Cebu Pacific's strength could continue to erode Capital A's market share in the Philippines, Citi says. Ongoing tightness in the aviation industry's supply chain may also pressure Capital A, driving Citi to lower its earnings estimates for 2025 and 2026 by 28% and 10%, respectively. Capital A's non-airline businesses' limited profitability track record and delayed restructuring plans also weigh on the stock, Citi says. Shares last closed at MYR0.805. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0947 GMT - Cathay Pacific's earnings growth will likely remain steady over the next two years despite ticket-pricing pressure and cargo headwinds, DBS Group Research says in a note. Passenger revenue growth, lower ex-fuel unit costs, cheaper fuel and higher contributions from associate Air China could drive core net profit through 2026, analysts Jason Sum and Tabitha Foo say. These factors will likely offset cargo headwinds amid trade tensions and further moderation in passenger yields as supply-demand dynamics normalize, they add. Cathay's 6%-7% dividend yield should help limit share-price downside, they write. DBS raises its target price on the stock to HK$12.20 from HK$12.00 while keeping a buy rating. Shares closed at HK$10.30. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0932 GMT - Rheinmetall's market share is set to grow significantly, supporting long-term revenue projections of between 34 billion and 70 billion euros by 2032, Mwb-research analyst Jens-Peter Rieck says. The German arms maker is at the forefront of catering for European defense needs, securing contracts wins over rival consortium KNDS and high-profile meetings with European leaders. CEO Armin Papperger was the only industrial chief holding direct talks with Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky during the Munich Security Conference, Rieck says. Rheinmetall is also demonstrating unmatched production efficiency, and its 2024 earnings call exceeded expectations, he notes. Mwb-research raised its target price for Rheinmetall shares to 1,990 euros from 1,280 euros, and kept its buy rating on the stock. Shares are up 0.5% at 1,271.5 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0914 GMT - Hesai Group's earnings could be stronger this year, given the sensor maker's determination to curb operating expenses, Daiwa analysts write in a note. China's mass-market car market may drive stronger demand for LiDAR products, further boosting the company's performance this year, they say. Daiwa forecasts shipments of 3 million units of advanced driver-assistance systems LiDAR sensors this year in China, mainly driven by mid- to high-end cars with price tags over 150,000 yuan. Meanwhile, the robotics sector could drive some upside surprise for Hesai, as LiDAR is necessary to help robots navigate and avoid obstacles when operating in a complicate space, the investment bank says. Daiwa maintains a buy rating for the stock, which last closed at $22.55. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Exicure Gets Patent in Australia for Potential Cancer Treatment Combination
data.description:
Exicure said late Thursday it received a patent in Australia covering its combination approach to cancer treatment, specifically targeting CXCR4 and GPCRx.

The patent supports the company's ongoing phase 2 clinical trial assessing the combination of GPC-100 and propranolol to treat multiple myeloma, a cancer of plasma cells.

data.title: UMG falls after Ackman's Pershing reduces its stake
data.description:
** Shares in UMG fall 10% after Bill Ackman's Pershing Square reduced its stake in the company raising 1.3 billion euros ($1.41 billion) from the sale

** Pershing is selling 50 million of UMG's ordinary shares at 26.60 euros each, a discount of 4.6% to Thursday's closing price of 27.90 euros

** UMG is set to conduct a U.S. share sale by September 15 as per an agreement with Pershing Square that requires the latter to sell $500 million in connection with a U.S. listing of UMG

** Pershing Square holds a 7.48% stake in UMG, according to LSEG data

** Shares in Bollore SE and Vivendi , which own a combined 27.79% stake in UMG, are down 3.7% and 5.4% respectively

** If losses hold, UMG stock will see its worst day since July 2024

($1 = 0.9233 euros)

data.title: US Natgas Prices Set for Weekly Fall
data.description:
US natural gas futures fell over 7% below $4.1/MMBtu this week, pressured by near-record output and forecasts for milder weather over the next two weeks.

Production in the Lower 48 states rose to 105.7 billion cubic feet per day (bcfd) in March, surpassing February's record of 105.1 bcfd.

However, gas flows to U.S. LNG export plants also reached 15.6 bcfd, matching last month's record.

The latest EIA report showed US utilities withdrew 62 billion cubic feet (bcf) of gas for the week ending March 7, exceeding forecasts of 50-55 bcf.

As a result, storage levels remain 27% lower than the same period last year and 11.9% below the five-year average.

data.title: Daimler Truck rises on solid 2025 guidance
data.description:
** Shares in Daimler Truck rise 3% after the German truckmaker gave a strong outlook for this year, and as Q4 earnings beat expectations

** The group guides for 2025 adjusted EBIT to rise by 5-15% and launches a 1 billion euro ($1.08 billion) cost-cutting programme until 2030

** "Robust performance and a solid guide," J.P.Morgan analysts write in a note to clients

** Daimler Truck shares are up 9.5% year-to-date

data.title: Formosa Plastics Incurs NT$1.23 Billion Loss in 2024
data.description:
Formosa Plastics incurred a loss attributable to owners of NT$1.23 billion, or NT$0.19 per share for 2024.

The PVC resins and other intermediate plastic products manufacturer did not provide comparative year-ago figures.

Operating revenue stood at NT$200 billion, according to a Thursday filing with Taiwan Stock Exchange.

data.title: Akobo Minerals Says February Gold Production At 1.3 Kg
data.description:
Akobo Minerals AB (publ) :

  • AKOBO MINERALS REPORTS FEBRUARY 2025 OPERATIONAL UPDATE

  • FEBRUARY GOLD PRODUCTION AT 1.3 KG, TOTAL TO DATE 18.6 KG

  • FEBRUARY GOLD PRODUCTION LOW DUE TO LOWER GRADE ORE

  • OPERATIONAL MINING PERFORMANCE REMAINED SOLID IN FEBRUARY

data.title: Italian Competition Watchdog Probes Eni, Renewables Unit Over Unfair Commercial Practices
data.description:
The Italian Competition Authority began investigating Eni's (ENI.MI) renewable arm Plenitude regarding its methods to inform users about the renewal of economic supply conditions.

The regulator said Friday it received "many" reports from consumers that the electricity and gas supply contracts were renewed with revised economic conditions without any prior notice, thus preventing them from exercising their right to a withdrawal. The complaints were received between May and September 2024.

The regulator's officials and the Italian Financial Police's Special Antitrust Unit inspected the premises of the Italian energy company and the subsidiary on Thursday, according to the notice.

data.title: Fluicell Is Granted EU Patent For 3D Biological Tissue Technology
data.description:
Fluicell AB :

  • GRANTED EU PATENT FOR 3D BIOLOGICAL TISSUE TECHNOLOGY

data.title: Hopewind Electric Flags 2024 Impairment of 108 Million Yuan
data.description:
Shenzhen Hopewind Electric flagged an impairment loss of 108.0 million yuan for 2024.

The loss includes 50.8 million in credit impairment and RMB 57.2 million in asset impairment, according to a Friday filing with the Shanghai bourse.
